---
title: "Composed distributions"
description: "Wire delays into a tree structure with Sequential, Parallel, Resolve, and Compete — one object that simulates a case and fits to observed ones."
toc: true
toc-location: left
---

:::::: {.side-space}

::: {.what-is-turing-grid .section-start-space .section-end-space}

::: {.feature-text-column}

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-composable"></span>
```

##### Composing trees of events

:::

A case's progression — onset, admission, death or discharge — is a tree of delays.
Compose them into one distribution that both simulates cases and fits to observed ones, with no separate model for each direction.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-uncertainty"></span>
```

##### Works with individual records

:::

Each line-list record contributes its own likelihood, and the tree fills in whichever delays weren't observed for that case.
The same object handles complete, censored, and partially observed records, with no aggregation step.

:::

::: {.feature-item}
::: {.feature-item-header}

```{=html}
<span class="feature-icon icon-response"></span>
```

##### Same representation, multiple uses

:::

The same tree works for delay distributions in renewal processes, for observation models in Turing fits, and for dropping into differential-equation models, with no translation step.

:::

::: {.feature-cta-button}

```{=html}
<a href="https://composeddistributions.epiaware.org/dev/" class="button btn-lg button--fill">Documentation &rarr;</a>
```

:::

:::

::: {.code-example-column}
::: {.code-window .box}
::: {.code-window-header}
::: {.code-window-dots}

```{=html}
<span class="dot red"></span><span class="dot yellow"></span><span class="dot green"></span>
```

:::

::: {.code-window-title}

```{=html}
<span>composed_delays.jl</span>
```

:::

:::

```{.julia .code-overflow-scroll}
using ComposedDistributions, Distributions

# BDBV-inspired delay tree: parallel pathways
# with Sequential, Resolve, and Compete nested together
tree = compose((
    clinical = sequential(
        :onset_admit => Gamma(1.2, 3.0),
        :admit_resolve => resolve(
            :death => (Gamma(2.0, 3.5), 0.3),
            :discharge => (Gamma(1.0, 8.0), 0.7))),
    surveillance = sequential(
        :onset_notif => Gamma(0.7, 20.0),
        :notif_compete => compete(
            :confirmed => Gamma(2.0, 1.0),
            :lost => Gamma(5.0, 0.5))),
))

# Inspect tree, params, and default priors
event_tree(tree)
params_table(tree)
priors = build_priors(params_table(tree))

# Draw a synthetic case, or evaluate a real one's likelihood
rand(tree)
logpdf(tree, ans)
```

:::
:::

:::

## The approach

A case's event history is a tree.
There is a root event (onset), a branch for each later event (admission, notification), and a split where the path forks (death or discharge).
Every node is a probability distribution over a delay, and the whole tree is itself a distribution.

The same object works in both directions.
`rand` draws a full event path from the tree; `logpdf` gives the likelihood of an observed case.
Both share one internal representation, so the model you build to simulate is the model you use to fit.

### Handles partial line-list data

Line-list data is sparse: some delays are observed, some are censored, some are missing entirely.
Each case supplies only the delays it has, and the tree accounts for the rest by integrating over the branches it did not see.
Because each case is handled on its own, the approach works directly on individual-level data — the composed object carries the censoring structure and applies it case by case.

The same object handles complete cases, censored cases, and partially observed cases.
The model does not change, only the delays each case supplies.

### One representation across the workflow

The tree is not tied to one package or one use.
The same object that defines a delay distribution for a renewal model drops into a Turing fit unchanged, and the same structure works for differential-equation models.
There is no translation step between the delay model, the infection model, and the observation model — the composed object is the shared vocabulary.

### Read the posterior back through the same tree

After fitting, the posterior is read back by applying the parameter draws to the same tree.
`update(template, chain)` walks each draw through the tree and returns a distribution per draw, so every delay mean, convolution, or contrast comes from the fitted object itself.
Nothing is recomputed in separate post-processing that could drift from the model.

## Where next

- The [ComposedDistributions.jl documentation](https://composeddistributions.epiaware.org/dev/)
  covers the full API and worked examples.
- [ConvolvedDistributions.jl](https://convolveddistributions.epiaware.org/) handles
  the convolutions (sums) of delays that arise naturally in event trees.
- [ModifiedDistributions.jl](https://modifieddistributions.epiaware.org/) lets you
  rescale, weight, or otherwise transform individual leaves without changing the
  tree structure.
- [CensoredDistributions.jl](https://censoreddistributions.epiaware.org/) adds
  the censoring layer for real line-list data.
- Ready to fit a full model with infection dynamics? See the
  [composable Turing models approach](composable-turing-models.qmd).

::::::
